title: How Boulder Creek Got Its Name and Who Was Here First
excerpt: The town is named after the stream.
bodyHtml:
The town is named after the stream. Boulder Creek, the waterway, comes down off the east side of Ben Lomond mountain and meets the San Lorenzo River at the north end of town, and the settlement that grew up near that junction took the creek's name when it got a post office in 1872. The creek's own name is about as literal as names get. Rail historian Derek Whaley, whose Santa Cruz Trains project documents this valley in detail, credits it to early trappers and lumbermen who wanted a plain, descriptive label for a rocky feeder creek of the San Lorenzo. No legend, no founder's ego, just boulders.

That's the short answer. The longer answer involves a sawmill man named Joseph Wilbur Peery, a rival town called Lorenzo that burned, and a railroad that made the name permanent. And before any of it, this was a lived-in landscape for thousands of years, which is where the story properly starts.

The people who were here first

People have lived in the Santa Cruz region for more than 10,000 years, according to the Santa Cruz Museum of Natural History, and the Indigenous people of this area spoke Awaswas, one of at least eight languages in what anthropologists later grouped together as the Ohlone language family. These weren't passing visitors. The museum describes communities that actively tended the landscape, managed resources through the seasons, and knew these mountains in a way no one since has matched.

Their descendants are still here, and they speak for themselves. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band is made up of the surviving descendant families of the tribal groups taken into Mission Santa Cruz, where Awaswas was spoken, and Mission San Juan Bautista, where Mutsun was spoken. The tribe counts nearly 600 enrolled members today. Through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust, they steward ancestral lands they know as Popeloutchom, running from Ano Nuevo along the ridgelines of the Santa Cruz Mountains to Monterey Bay. That work is active right now, with programs like the Native Stewardship Corps working in these mountains.

One honest caveat: written records for the upper San Lorenzo Valley specifically are thin, and this article won't pretend to name the exact village communities that lived along this stretch of river. What's well documented is that Awaswas-speaking people lived throughout the region for millennia before a single tree was milled, and that their descendants never left the story.

A creek named for what's in it

Whaley's dictionary of local place names keeps the entry simple: the town "refers to Boulder Creek, a year-round tributary of the San Lorenzo River" and was named after the creek. The creek itself got its name from the trappers and lumbermen who worked the valley early on, and anyone who has looked at the streambed understands why. It's a boulder-choked mountain creek, and the men naming things around here weren't poets.

The geography matters more than it might seem. Boulder Creek and Bear Creek both meet the San Lorenzo River close together at the north end of the valley floor. When railroad planners later mapped the area, they called that three-way junction of waterways "the Turkey Foot," a detail preserved in Whaley's history of the Felton & Pescadero Railroad. That confluence made this spot the natural gathering point for the upper valley's timber, which is why a town formed here at all.

Boulder before Boulder Creek

The first Anglo settlement here didn't start with a grand plan. Joseph Wilbur Peery put up a small sawmill across from Bear Creek in the early 1860s, and a modest settlement called Boulder grew around the milling activity. Peery eventually moved on, and the Crediford family kept the little settlement going.

The name change came through the mail. In 1872 a post office opened inside the general store, and it was registered as Boulder Creek. Post office names had a way of becoming official names in rural California, since the postmark was often the only piece of paper that regularly said where you were. From 1872 on, the place was Boulder Creek in the eyes of the federal government, whatever the locals called it day to day.

Lorenzo, the town that almost won

Here's the twist most newcomers haven't heard: Boulder Creek had a rival a mile down the road, founded by the same man who built the first mill. In January 1875, Peery established a town he named Lorenzo, after the river, on the county road south of Boulder. Whaley's history of the site records that Lorenzo grew into a real village with hotels, saloons, and a town hall, sitting between today's Boulder Creek and Brookdale.

For a moment, Lorenzo even looked like the winner. When the railroad built up the valley in 1885, a station at Lorenzo briefly served as the end of the line while crews finished the last stretch north. But the permanent terminus went in at Boulder Creek, closer to the timber and the Turkey Foot, and the railroad's choice decided the contest. Southern Pacific renamed the Lorenzo station Filbert in 1887, probably for the California hazel tree, mostly to avoid confusion with other stations carrying the Lorenzo name.

The end came quickly after that. In 1897 a kitchen fire spread through Lorenzo and destroyed the two major hotels, the town hall, and other buildings along the county road. Peery made a brief attempt at rebuilding, then gave it up, and Lorenzo was absorbed into Boulder Creek. One building from the Lorenzo era outlived the town: the original Grace Episcopal Church, which the San Lorenzo Valley Historical Society notes stood "in what was originally the town of Lorenzo." That church now houses the historical society's museum gallery at 12547 Highway 9, so the best surviving piece of Lorenzo sits in downtown Boulder Creek.

The railroad makes it permanent

The name question was settled for good in April 1885, when the first train rolled into the new Boulder Creek terminus and, as Whaley puts it, the prosperity of Boulder Creek truly began. Railroads printed timetables, painted station signs, and shipped freight under one name, and that kind of repetition ends arguments. Boulder faded into Boulder Creek, Lorenzo burned and folded in, and the post office name from 1872 became the only name left standing.

Everything that followed, the lumber boom, the resort era, the quiet decades after the trains stopped, happened under that name. If you want the full sweep of what came next, our short history of Boulder Creek picks up the story from here.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Boulder Creek named after the creek or the boulders?

Both, in sequence. The creek was named first, by early trappers and lumbermen who gave a rocky stream an obvious, descriptive name. The town then took its name from the creek by way of the post office that opened in 1872. So the town is named after the creek, and the creek is named after what's sitting in it.

What was the town of Lorenzo?

Lorenzo was a village Joseph Wilbur Peery founded in January 1875 about a mile south of Boulder Creek, named for the San Lorenzo River. It had hotels, a town hall, and its own railroad station for a time. A kitchen fire destroyed most of it in 1897, and the site was absorbed into Boulder Creek. Its old Grace Episcopal Church survives as the San Lorenzo Valley Museum's gallery on Highway 9.

Who lived in the San Lorenzo Valley before settlers arrived?

Awaswas-speaking peoples, part of the broader group anthropologists call Ohlone, lived throughout the Santa Cruz region for more than 10,000 years. Their descendants belong to the Amah Mutsun Tribal Band, which works today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust to restore Indigenous stewardship across ancestral lands that include the Santa Cruz Mountains.

Was Boulder Creek ever called something else?

The settlement was originally just Boulder, named for the creek beside it. The post office established in 1872 carried the name Boulder Creek, and once the railroad arrived in 1885 with a station of the same name, Boulder Creek was the name that stuck.

title: The Essential Shops: What You Can Actually Buy in Town
excerpt: You can cover more errands in Boulder Creek than most people expect from a town this size: hardware and lumber, two full grocery stores, an independent pharmacy with free valley-wide delivery, a full-service vet, and a surprising bench of gift and specialty shops, all inside about a mile of Highway 9.
bodyHtml:
You can cover more errands in Boulder Creek than most people expect from a town this size: hardware and lumber, two full grocery stores, an independent pharmacy with free valley-wide delivery, a full-service vet, and a surprising bench of gift and specialty shops, all inside about a mile of Highway 9. What follows is a needs-based tour, not a ranking, and every business named here was confirmed operating against its own site or a current official listing on July 15, 2026. Hours shift with seasons and staffing up here, so check before you make a special trip. For everything in town beyond the essentials, the full directory is the master list.

Hardware, lumber, and the endless mountain-house fix

Two related stores split this territory. Scarborough Home & Garden at 13110 Highway 9 is the in-town hardware stop, an Ace Hardware affiliate stocking tools, paint, garden and outdoor supplies, and brands like DeWalt, Milwaukee, EGO, and Weber. It's open seven days: Monday through Saturday 8 to 5 and Sunday 9 to 5.

For lumber and heavier building materials, Scarborough Building Supply runs the yard at 12990 Highway 9, open weekdays 7:30 to 6 with shorter weekend hours. Between the two, most repair-and-project runs never need to leave the valley.

Groceries: two real markets, different personalities

Boulder Creek supports two full grocery stores a couple of blocks apart, which is its own kind of small-town luxury.

Johnnie's Super Market at 13225 Highway 9 has anchored the north end of downtown since 1938, and it's a complete market: produce sourced locally where possible, a meat and seafood counter, a deli making sandwiches daily, a bakery case, and a deep wine, spirits, and beer selection. It opens at 8 a.m. every day, running to 9 p.m. most nights and 10 on Friday and Saturday.

Wild Roots Market at 13159 Central Avenue is the natural-foods counterpart, locally owned since 1991, with organic produce, a deli and kitchen, and baked goods from its Sunflour Bakehouse. It's open daily 9 to 9. There's a second Wild Roots down the valley in Felton, but the Boulder Creek store is its own full market.

Between them, the practical grocery question in town isn't "can I get it here" so much as "which store carries my version of it."

Pharmacy: independent, and it delivers

Boulder Creek Pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue has served the town for over 40 years as a locally owned, family-operated pharmacy. Beyond prescriptions and over-the-counter basics, it handles medication synchronization and multi-dose packaging, and it offers free delivery throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, which matters a lot on this side of the hill when a car is in the shop or a storm has the roads a mess. Hours are Monday through Friday 9 to 6 and Saturday 10 to 4, closed Sunday. The phone is 831-338-2144.

Pets and gardens

There's no standalone pet store in town, but Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic at 12870 Highway 9 covers the animal side thoroughly: a full-service clinic with surgery, dental, boarding, exotic pet care, and an online pharmacy, open six days a week including two evenings. The phone is 831-338-7205.

For garden needs, Scarborough Home & Garden carries outdoor and garden supplies alongside its hardware lines, and Good Vibes, covered below, leans into houseplants.

Gifts, style, and the specialty row

Downtown's specialty shops cluster along a few blocks of Highway 9, and they're more varied than a first drive-through suggests.

Air & Fire, the "mystical bazaar" at 13150 Highway 9, stocks handmade soaps, candles, incense, crystals, jewelry, tarot decks, books, and local art, and hosts classes and readings; it's open daily with seasonal hours posted on its site. SWAG at 13026 Highway 9 mixes restored mid-century stereos, vinyl, vintage apparel, and local art and jewelry inside an 1893 building the shop notes was the first US commercial building owned and operated by women; the business association lists it open Tuesday through Saturday afternoons. SWAG also carries select items from Stumptown Supply, a Boulder Creek-born online brand making apparel and gifts around San Lorenzo Valley history, so you can see the goods in person even though the brand itself has no storefront.

Ginia's Gems at 13133 Highway 9 sells handmade memory-wire jewelry with semi-precious stones plus herbal remedies and local honey; call ahead for current hours. Boulder Creek Antiques at Mac's Place, at 13164 Highway 9, deals in furniture, collectibles, and vintage finds, and the business association's current listing shows it open daily in the afternoon range. The same listing covers Wild Lilith, an independent boutique at 13120 Highway 9 with limited weekday hours, and Good Vibes at 13124 Highway 9, a newer shop carrying plants, clothing, and locally made goods.

And in a genuine small-town plot twist, there's a shoe store: Beck's Shoes runs a discount showroom at 12550 Highway 9 at the south end of town, open Thursday through Sunday, 10 to 6, selling premium work, outdoor, and casual footwear at reduced prices, in-store only.

Cannabis, for adults 21 and over

Boulder Creek also has a licensed adult-use dispensary. Creekside Wellness at 12603 Highway 9 has operated since 2018 under state license C10-0000008-LIC, open Monday through Saturday 10 to 7 and Sunday noon to 6. It's a 21-and-over business, ID required.

What this list is, and isn't

This is the errand-running core: the places that keep a household fed, medicated, repaired, and occasionally gift-wrapped without a trip over the hill. It isn't a ranking, and it isn't everything. Boulder Creek's cafes, restaurants, and bars get their own guide in where to eat in Boulder Creek, services from plumbers to salons live in the directory, and if you've just moved here, the new resident quick-start guide puts these stops in week-one order. If we've missed a shop or a detail has changed, the directory page is the place to tell us.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a hardware store in Boulder Creek?

Yes, two related ones. Scarborough Home & Garden at 13110 Highway 9 is an Ace-affiliated hardware and garden store open seven days a week, and Scarborough Building Supply at 12990 Highway 9 handles lumber and building materials.

Does Boulder Creek have a real grocery store?

It has two. Johnnie's Super Market, a full-line independent grocery in business since 1938, and Wild Roots Market, a natural-foods grocery with a deli and bakery, sit within a few blocks of each other downtown, both open daily.

Is there a pharmacy in town?

Yes. Boulder Creek Pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue is independent and family-operated, open weekdays and Saturdays, with free prescription delivery throughout the San Lorenzo Valley.

Where can I buy gifts locally?

The downtown blocks of Highway 9 hold most of it: Air & Fire for candles, crystals, and artisan goods, SWAG for vinyl, vintage, and local art, Ginia's Gems for handmade jewelry, Boulder Creek Antiques for vintage finds, and Wild Lilith and Good Vibes for boutique goods and plants. Hours vary by shop, so check before a special trip.

What will I still need to drive down the hill for?

Big-box selection, mostly. The town covers hardware, groceries, pharmacy, shoes, gifts, and pet care through the vet, but department-store shopping and specialty chains mean a run to Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz. The full local list lives in our directory.

title: "Railroads of the San Lorenzo Valley: The Line That Built Boulder Creek"
excerpt: Trains ran to Boulder Creek for just under fifty years, from April 1885 to January 1934, and the town you drive through today is largely the town they built.
bodyHtml:
Trains ran to Boulder Creek for just under fifty years, from April 1885 to January 1934, and the town you drive through today is largely the town they built. The narrow-gauge branch up the valley from Felton hauled out the lumber that made Boulder Creek one of the busiest shipping points in the redwoods, brought in the tourists who replaced the loggers, and then quietly disappeared, leaving concrete piers in the river and a right-of-way you can still trace in places along Highway 9. The most complete record of all of it is rail historian Derek Whaley's Santa Cruz Trains project, which this article leans on throughout.

Before the trains: a flume that couldn't keep up

The railroad exists because a wooden trough failed. In August 1874 a flume company incorporated to move the upper valley's lumber, and by October 1875 its V-shaped flume was floating rough-cut boards roughly twelve miles from the headwaters of the San Lorenzo down to Felton. Whaley's history of the company reads like a punch list of problems: the 40-inch trough couldn't handle the valley's output, it leaked, its water ran dangerously low every summer, and a winter storm in early 1876 tore it up badly.

In 1879 the South Pacific Coast Railroad took over the flume company. The South Pacific Coast was the ambitious narrow-gauge line that, per the Santa Cruz Trains FAQ, opened its route through the Santa Cruz Mountains in 1880, connecting Santa Cruz to San Francisco Bay in about four hours, ferry ride from Alameda Point included. Once that mainline was running through Felton, replacing the leaky flume with a branch up the valley was the obvious next move.

The Felton & Pescadero Railroad, 1883 to 1885

The branch was born on paper as something grander. On June 13, 1883, the South Pacific Coast incorporated a subsidiary called the Felton & Pescadero Railroad, with a first stage running up the San Lorenzo Valley and a second stage meant to push on toward Pescadero. Grading the narrow-gauge line took nearly two years. The route bypassed downtown Felton, followed the river north, and aimed for a clearing just south of the spot where the San Lorenzo meets Boulder and Bear creeks, a three-way junction the railroad called the Turkey Foot.

During construction, the end of track sat briefly at Lorenzo, the small rival town a mile south of Boulder Creek, and Lorenzo's station served as a temporary terminus. But the permanent terminal went in at Boulder Creek, and when the first train rolled into town in April 1885, Whaley writes, the prosperity of Boulder Creek truly began. The flume was demolished along the new route. The Pescadero half of the name never happened; the tracks never went further north than the logging country above town.

The corporate name on the locomotives changed almost immediately. On May 23, 1887, the South Pacific Coast consolidated its railroads into the South Pacific Coast Railway, and on July 1 of that year the whole system was leased to Southern Pacific. Under SP, the line became first the Felton Branch and eventually the Boulder Creek Branch, and after the 1906 earthquake (which did little damage in Boulder Creek) the tracks were converted from narrow gauge to standard gauge.

End of the line: Boulder Creek's railroad years

For three decades the branch was a lumber conveyor. Boulder Creek ranked, in Whaley's words, as one of the largest exporters of lumber in the United States from 1885 to 1915, and the town's year-round population passed 800 on the strength of the freight yard at the center of it.

The branch also carried people, and the string of little stations between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek tells you who they were. Whaley's route survey lists stops like Phillipshurst, established for a Dr. Phillips; Siesta, set up near Brookdale at the urging of promoter Fred Swanton; Fish Hatchery, serving the state facility at Brookdale; Harris, which catered to Camp Joy; and Filbert, the renamed Lorenzo station near the end of Grove Street, which served the Redwood Rest Resort. Between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek the line hugged the west bank of the San Lorenzo and crossed the river at least seven times, running for one stretch in a cut between River Road and the water.

One place the trains never went: Big Basin. After the park was established in 1902, proposals to reach it by rail came and went for years, and Whaley's Big Basin history is direct about the outcome: rail access never came. Southern Pacific surveyed toward Pescadero Creek in 1905 with an eye on a park branch, but the 1906 earthquake shelved the plan. Visitors instead rode stages, and as early as 1886 the Moody and Cress livery stable in Boulder Creek was hauling picnickers and campers up toward the basin. The rail-to-stagecoach transfer made Boulder Creek the gateway town, a role it never gave up.

The Dougherty Extension: the loggers' own railroad

North of town, the rails kept going, but they didn't belong to Southern Pacific. After the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company's mill burned in 1886, brothers James and William P. Dougherty built their own narrow-gauge line north from the Boulder Creek freight yard rather than wait for an official extension. Construction began in the late winter of 1887, and the Dougherty Extension Railroad eventually ran roughly eight and a half miles up the San Lorenzo, reaching a bend of the river below Castle Rock Ridge by 1899. Spurs served the Morrell mill at Two Bar Creek, Cunningham & Company, and the S. H. Chase Lumber Company.

The Dougherty line was a freight operation with one charming exception. Starting May 19, 1909, a real estate promoter borrowed the line's little locomotive, known as the Dinkey, to shuttle prospective lot buyers up to the new Wildwood subdivision, and the summer runs continued for five seasons. Regular service on the line ended around 1915, and on April 4, 1917, the rails were sold for scrap, pulling the railroad's northern reach out of the valley for good.

Decline and the last train

The branch outlived the logging boom by about two decades, but the arithmetic only got worse. Regularly scheduled evening freight service ended in 1921. In March 1931, Southern Pacific replaced the branch's two daily passenger trains with Pacific Greyhound buses. And in January 1934 the last freight train left Boulder Creek, closing the town's railroad era after 49 years. Whaley's summary is that the town transitioned begrudgingly from a lumber town to a tourist town, a shift our short history of Boulder Creek traces in full.

The valley's rail connection to the outside world didn't last much longer. A severe storm on February 26, 1940 wrecked the old mountain mainline between Santa Cruz and Los Gatos, and Southern Pacific won formal abandonment that November. With that, the age of trains in the San Lorenzo Valley effectively closed.

Tracing the line today

You can still find the railroad if you know where to look. In Boulder Creek, the station grounds sat just behind today's fire department, at the heart of the downtown blocks the freight yard once dominated. In Brookdale, Whaley points to the bottom of Pacific Street as the most accessible piece of the old alignment. Along the river between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek, concrete piers and sawed-off pilings from the line's many crossings still stand in and beside the water.

A word of caution before you go hunting: much of the old right-of-way crosses private property, so treat road crossings and public riverbanks as your vantage points rather than walking the grade. For photographs, timetables, and the deeper archive, the San Lorenzo Valley Museum at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek keeps the valley's rail history, and Whaley's Santa Cruz Trains site documents nearly every station, bridge, and spur if you want to go deeper still.

Frequently Asked Questions

When did trains stop running to Boulder Creek?

Passenger service ended in March 1931, when Southern Pacific replaced the two daily trains with Pacific Greyhound buses. The last freight train left Boulder Creek in January 1934, ending 49 years of rail service that began in April 1885.

Did the railroad ever reach Big Basin?

No. Proposals circulated for decades, and Southern Pacific surveyed a possible route in 1905, but the 1906 earthquake shelved the plan and no rail line was ever built into the park. Visitors transferred to stagecoaches in Boulder Creek and Ben Lomond, which is how Boulder Creek became the gateway to Big Basin.

Why was the line called the Felton & Pescadero Railroad?

The 1883 charter imagined a line that would continue past Boulder Creek to Pescadero. The first stage, Felton to Boulder Creek, opened in April 1885, but the extension was never built, and the removal of the logging tracks north of town in 1917 ended the idea permanently.

Can I walk the old railroad grade?

Mostly no. Much of the right-of-way between Felton and Boulder Creek now runs through private property. The practical way to see the line's remains is from public spots: the bottom of Pacific Street in Brookdale, road crossings along Highway 9 and River Road, and the riverbanks where concrete piers and old pilings are visible.

What was the Dougherty Extension?

A private narrow-gauge logging railroad built north from Boulder Creek starting in 1887 by the Dougherty brothers of the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company. It grew to roughly eight and a half miles, served several mills, briefly carried summer passengers to the Wildwood subdivision starting in 1909, and was scrapped in 1917.

title: How Directory Verification Works (and How to Fix a Listing)
excerpt: Here's how the Boulder Creek Local directory works, in one paragraph. Every listing is reviewed by a person before it's published.
bodyHtml:
Here's how the Boulder Creek Local directory works, in one paragraph. Every listing is reviewed by a person before it's published. Each entry carries a verification date and a confidence level, and we recheck listings on a schedule. Being listed is informational, not an endorsement, and it's never something a business pays for. If a listing is wrong, tell us through the form at /submit and a person will check it against a first-party source before anything changes.

The rest of this page explains what each of those sentences actually means, because a directory is only useful if you know how much to trust it.

Where listing information comes from

We prefer official and first-party sources, in this order of habit: official agency, district, school, park, and utility pages; a business or organization's own website; an official business social profile when no maintained website exists; and current licensing or registration records for regulated services.

Association directories, maps, review sites, and older local directories are useful for finding candidates, and that's all we use them for. They aren't treated as proof that a business is open or that its details are current. Anyone who has searched for a Boulder Creek restaurant and landed on a listing for a place that closed years ago knows why that rule exists.

What a person does before a listing goes live

Every listing is reviewed by a human before it's published. Automation helps us collect and organize, but nothing appears in the directory because a script decided it should.

For each entry we record two things: a verification date, meaning the date a person last confirmed the details against a source, and a confidence level, our own note on how solid the sourcing is. Then we recheck on a schedule. Things that change fast, like restaurant hours and emergency resources, get rechecked more often than stable resident resources.

When we can't confirm a detail, we mark it as unavailable. We never treat missing information as an all clear. If a listing doesn't show hours, that means we couldn't verify hours, not that the business is always open.

What the verified date means, and what it doesn't

The verified date on a listing tells you when a person last confirmed the entry against a first-party source. It's a snapshot, not a promise. Hours, availability, prices, and services change, especially in a mountain town where weather, power, and staffing all take their turns. For anything time sensitive, confirm directly with the business or agency before you drive.

We never guarantee safety, availability, quality, admission, access, or outcome. That's not fine print for its own sake. It's the limit of what any directory can know.

What we deliberately leave out

Some absences are on purpose:

We don't rank or score businesses. There's no "best of Boulder Creek" here, no stars, and no ordering that rewards anyone.

We don't publish private home addresses. Home-based businesses are listed without one.

We don't list individual informal caregivers, and we don't list unverified emergency contractors. Those are categories where a wrong or stale listing can do real harm.

Money never changes a listing

A factual listing in this directory is free, and it stays free. We don't charge businesses to be listed, and we don't accept payment to change facts, placement, or verification.

The site may earn revenue to keep running, and the rules for that are strict. Sponsored content, sponsored placements, and advertisements are clearly labeled wherever they appear, and editorial listings and guides are kept separate from paid placements. We won't sell higher rankings, better reviews, or preferential facts. We won't present a paid message as independent editorial content. And we won't remove or downgrade a competitor's accurate listing because someone paid us. Every business is held to the same sourcing standard whether or not it ever pays us anything.

One more time, because it's the line everything else hangs on: inclusion in the directory is informational. It is not an endorsement.

How to fix a listing

If something is wrong, out of date, or shouldn't be here, we want to know. Business owners and their authorized representatives can ask, and so can any resident or visitor who spots an error. You don't need to be connected to the business to flag a problem.

The fastest route is the submit form. A useful request includes five things: the listing or page (a link is easiest), what's wrong or what you'd like changed, a first-party source we can use to confirm the correct information (an official website, an agency page, or a licensing record), your name and how you're connected to the business or content, and a way to reach you.

Here's what happens next. We acknowledge your request. We check the correct information against a first-party or official source, not against the request alone. Then we update, remove, or explain our decision, and a person reviews the change before it's published. Safety-related corrections are handled first.

Two special cases. A business may ask to be removed from the directory, and we'll remove the listing; where official or public-record information matters to residents, like an agency, utility, or public-safety contact, we may keep that information and note its official source. And if you believe content or an image infringes your copyright, trademark, or other rights, send the item, the rights you hold, and enough detail for us to locate it, and we'll remove or correct anything that shouldn't have been published.

One thing this process is not: an emergency channel. In an emergency, call 911.

The full policies behind this page, including sourcing, corrections, and the sponsorship disclosure, live at /legal.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is being listed an endorsement?

No. Inclusion is informational only. A listing means a person verified, on the date shown, that the business exists and the recorded details matched a first-party source. It says nothing about quality, and we don't rank or score anyone.

Can a business pay for a better listing?

No. Factual listings are free, and payment never changes facts, placement, description, or verification. If we ever run sponsored placements or ads, they're labeled as such and kept separate from editorial listings.

Why does a listing say a detail is unavailable?

Because we couldn't verify it against a source we trust. We'd rather show a gap than guess. Unavailable never means "probably fine," so for time-sensitive plans, contact the business directly.

How do I correct my business's listing?

Use the form at /submit. Include the listing, what needs to change, and a first-party source we can verify against, plus your name, your connection to the business, and a way to reach you. A person checks the source and reviews the change before it goes live, and safety-related fixes jump the line.

Can I have my business removed?

Yes. Ask through /submit and we'll remove the listing. The one exception is official or public-record information residents rely on, such as agency or utility contacts, which we may keep with its official source noted.

title: Getting Around Without a Car: Transit Options From Boulder Creek
excerpt: Yes, you can get from Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz on a bus, seven days a week.
bodyHtml:
Yes, you can get from Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz on a bus, seven days a week. Santa Cruz METRO's Route 35 runs the length of Highway 9 through Felton and Scotts Valley to downtown Santa Cruz, a weekday express called Route 35X makes the same trip faster during commute hours, and a local ride costs $2 in cash, with K-12 students riding free. For neighbors who can't use a regular bus, METRO ParaCruz and Lift Line both provide door-to-door rides. Everything below was checked against the live official pages on July 15, 2026. Bus schedules change with each service update, so treat the linked timetables as the source of truth and this page as the map.

Route 35 is the backbone

Route 35, the Highway 9/Scotts Valley line, is how Boulder Creek connects to the rest of the county without a car. It runs between the RiverFront transit stops on Front Street in downtown Santa Cruz and the Country Club end of Big Basin Way, passing through Felton and Scotts Valley on the way.

In and around town, the schedule's timepoints include Highway 9 at Middleton Avenue in the middle of downtown, Pool Drive at Highway 9 by the Mountain Store, and Big Basin Way out toward the Country Club neighborhood. Heading down the hill, buses stop at San Lorenzo Valley High School and Highlands Park before continuing through Felton.

Service runs seven days a week, from early morning until late evening, with buses roughly every 30 to 60 minutes through the middle of the day. Don't build a tight connection around those generalities, though. Pull up the live Route 35 timetable for the exact times at your stop, because first and last trips differ by direction and day of week.

If you're new in town and still setting up the rest of your life here, our new resident quick-start guide covers the non-bus logistics.

Route 35X, the commuter's shortcut

On weekdays only, METRO runs Route 35X, the Santa Cruz/Boulder Creek Express. It serves the same Boulder Creek timepoints, including Highway 9 and Middleton and Pool Drive at the Mountain Store, but takes a quicker routing on the trip to and from Santa Cruz. The current schedule shows roughly a dozen trips in each direction spread across the workday, starting around 6:30 in the morning.

If you commute to Santa Cruz on a regular clock, the 35X is the one to learn. Check the Route 35X schedule for the exact departures, since express trips are the first thing to shift when METRO adjusts service.

What it costs

Fares are the simple part, and they're posted on METRO's Fares & Passes page:

A single local ride is $2.00 cash for adults, or $1.00 for riders 65 and older and riders with disabilities. A day pass runs $6, and a 31-day pass is $65, with discount versions at $3 and $32.

The best deal in the system is for students. K-12 riders travel free through the Youth Cruz Free program, which makes Route 35 a genuine option for SLV High and middle school students heading up and down the valley. College students with a valid student ID also ride free, and so do riders who are legally blind.

Getting to San Jose and over the hill

There's no direct bus from Boulder Creek over Highway 17, but the connection is straightforward. Ride Route 35 to its downtown Santa Cruz end at the RiverFront stops on Front Street. The Highway 17 Express boards a short walk away in the same RiverFront area, at Front Street near Trader Joe's, and runs weekdays and weekends to Diridon Station in San Jose, where Caltrain, Amtrak, and light rail all connect.

The Highway 17 Express uses its own fare: $7.00 for a single ride, $3.50 discount, or a $14 day pass. If you're commuting to the South Bay a few days a week, do the math on passes before you settle into paying cash each way.

Weekend bus to Big Basin

On weekends, Route 35B runs from the Cavallaro Transit Center in Scotts Valley up Highway 9 and out to Big Basin Redwoods State Park, with five trips in each direction. Check the route page for its full stop list before planning around a specific boarding point. If you're headed that way, our Big Basin day trip guide covers what to expect once you're there, including the park's parking-reservation advice, which makes the bus a smarter play than it might sound.

Door-to-door rides: ParaCruz and Lift Line

Two services cover trips a fixed-route bus can't.

METRO ParaCruz is METRO's ADA paratransit: accessible, shared door-to-door rides for people who can't use regular buses because of a disability. It serves destinations within three-quarters of a mile of an operating bus route, which in our corner of the county means the Highway 9 corridor. You apply through ADAride, online at adaride.com or by phone at 877-232-7433, and a decision comes within 21 days. Once you're eligible, rides are booked one to three days ahead at 831-425-4664, and the base fare is $4 per trip.

Lift Line is run by Community Bridges, a local nonprofit, and provides door-to-door rides for seniors 60 and older and people with disabilities across Santa Cruz County, with a focus on medical trips, meal-site rides, and essential errands. Some of its programs carry income qualifications, and rides need to be scheduled in advance. Start with the application on its site or call 831-688-9663 on a weekday to sort out which program fits.

If a ride is about a medical appointment, ask both services what applies to you. The eligibility rules differ, and plenty of valley residents qualify for one and not the other.

School buses for SLVUSD students

San Lorenzo Valley Unified runs its own home-to-school buses, separate from METRO, and posts route sheets by bus name on its Transportation Department bus information page. Ridership is fee-based: as posted for the current cycle, an annual pass is $220 round trip or $116 one way, a semester pass is $116 or $58, and ticket books run $20 for 20 tickets. Students with qualifying IEPs are exempt from fees, and there's a hardship application for families receiving county assistance. The transportation office in Ben Lomond answers questions at 831-336-2223. Confirm prices and routes with the district before the school year starts, since both get updated over the summer.

Remember the free alternative for older kids: K-12 students ride METRO at no cost, and Route 35 stops at SLV High.

A few honest caveats

The bus works well here, with two limits worth knowing before you give up a car entirely. Frequency is the first: miss a midday bus and the next one can be 30 to 60 minutes out. Storm season is the second: when Highway 9 closes for trees or slides, buses reroute or hold just like everyone else, so sign up for alerts through CruzAware and check METRO's rider alerts on the route pages before heading out in bad weather.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a bus from Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz?

Yes. METRO Route 35 runs seven days a week along Highway 9 from Boulder Creek through Felton and Scotts Valley to downtown Santa Cruz, and the weekday Route 35X express makes the same trip with a faster routing during commute hours.

How much does the bus cost from Boulder Creek?

A local single ride is $2.00 cash, with a $1.00 fare for riders 65 and older and riders with disabilities. Day passes are $6 and 31-day passes are $65. K-12 students, college students with ID, and riders who are legally blind ride free.

Can I get from Boulder Creek to San Jose by bus?

Yes, with one transfer. Take Route 35 to the RiverFront stops in downtown Santa Cruz, then board the Highway 17 Express to Diridon Station in San Jose. The express runs weekdays and weekends and costs $7.00 for a single ride.

What if I can't ride a regular bus?

Two door-to-door options serve Boulder Creek. METRO ParaCruz covers riders whose disability prevents using fixed-route buses, with an application through ADAride and $4 base fares. Lift Line, run by Community Bridges, serves seniors 60 and older and people with disabilities, with some income-qualified programs.

Do school kids ride METRO free?

Yes. The Youth Cruz Free program lets K-12 students ride any METRO route at no cost, no pass purchase required. SLVUSD's own yellow-bus service is separate and fee-based, with passes sold through the district's transportation office.

title: "The Logging Boom: When Boulder Creek Ran on Timber"
excerpt: For about half a century, Boulder Creek existed for one reason: cutting redwood and getting it out of the mountains.
bodyHtml:
For about half a century, Boulder Creek existed for one reason: cutting redwood and getting it out of the mountains. Rail historian Derek Whaley, whose Santa Cruz Trains project is the best-documented account of this era, writes that Boulder Creek was one of the largest exporters of lumber in the United States from 1885 to 1915. That's not small-town boosterism about a small town. It's the reason the town is here, and the near-loss of the forest around it is the reason Big Basin exists. This is the story of the mills, the flume, and the trains, and what's left of them today.

The forest before the saws

Start with what the loggers found. The Santa Cruz Mountains held roughly 300 square miles of ancient redwood forest, and the Sempervirens Fund puts it bluntly: in the late 1800s, most of it was "logged down to bald hills." Trees a thousand years old and older came down in a few decades.

That forest wasn't wilderness in the empty sense. People have lived in this region for more than 10,000 years, according to the Santa Cruz Museum of Natural History, and the Awaswas-speaking peoples here actively tended the landscape rather than simply occupying it. Their descendants in the Amah Mutsun Tribal Band steward ancestral lands in these mountains today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust. The logging era wasn't the beginning of human history in this valley. It was a violent interruption of a much longer one.

First mills on the upper river

Commercial logging reached the upper San Lorenzo Valley in the early 1860s, when Joseph Wilbur Peery put up a small sawmill across from Bear Creek. A settlement called Boulder grew around the milling work, got a post office named Boulder Creek in 1872, and kept that name. The early operations were small and local by necessity. A mill in the 1860s could cut lumber all day, but getting it down a rough mountain road to Santa Cruz was slow, expensive work, and that bottleneck kept the upper valley quiet for its first decade.

The flume years, 1875 to 1885

The first serious attempt to break the bottleneck was a wooden trough. A flume company incorporated in August 1874 (Whaley's pages render the name as both the San Lorenzo Valley Flume & Lumber Company and the Flume & Transportation Company) began construction in early 1875, and by that October a V-shaped flume was carrying rough-cut lumber roughly twelve miles from the upper San Lorenzo down to Felton, where it met the railroad to Santa Cruz. The company built a large mill near the confluence of Kings Creek and the San Lorenzo River, upstream of town, and Whaley's company history records that the flume turned the little settlement of Boulder into a much more substantial Boulder Creek.

It also records the flume's problems, which showed up almost immediately. The 40-inch trough couldn't keep up with the valley's lumber output, it leaked, and its water supply ran dangerously low every summer. A severe winter storm in early 1876 damaged it badly. In 1879 the South Pacific Coast Railroad took over the flume company, and in June 1883 it incorporated a subsidiary, the Felton & Pescadero Railroad, to replace the flume with rails. When the narrow-gauge line reached Boulder Creek in April 1885, crews demolished the flume along the route. The trough era lasted just ten years.

The boom, 1885 to 1915

The railroad changed the scale of everything. With the first train in April 1885, Boulder Creek became the end of the line and the shipping point for every mill working the upper valley, and the town's year-round population climbed past 800. Lumber went out by the trainload for thirty years, the stretch when Whaley ranks Boulder Creek among the largest lumber exporters in the country.

The clearest picture of how industrial it got is the railroad the loggers built themselves. After the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company's mill burned in 1886, brothers James and William P. Dougherty didn't wait for Southern Pacific to extend the branch. Starting in the late winter of 1887 they built their own narrow-gauge line, the Dougherty Extension Railroad, north from the Boulder Creek freight yard along the San Lorenzo River. By 1899 it ran roughly eight and a half miles upriver, into the shadow of Castle Rock Ridge, longer than the Southern Pacific branch it connected to. Spurs served a string of operations: Ephraim Bradbury Morrell's mill at Two Bar Creek, Cunningham & Company, and the S. H. Chase Lumber Company among them.

Demand never let up for long. The San Lorenzo Valley Chamber of Commerce notes that the whole valley ran as a logging center in these years, and that San Francisco's rebuilding after the 1906 earthquake pushed demand for local lumber even higher. The quake that leveled the city barely damaged Boulder Creek, and the town spent the next several years shipping the redwood that rebuilt it.

Running out of trees

The boom ended the way every cut-out-and-get-out boom ends. By the early 1900s the big timber within reach of the rails was going fast, and mills began closing as the accessible stands were exhausted. The Dougherty line's regular service wound down around 1915, and on April 4, 1917, its rails were sold for scrap, feeding the steel demand of the First World War. Downriver, the Southern Pacific branch hung on with resort passengers and lighter freight, but the direction was set. Passenger trains gave way to buses in 1931, and in January 1934 the last freight train left Boulder Creek.

Whaley's one-line summary of what followed is the fairest one: the town "transitioned begrudgingly from a lumber town to a tourist town." The mills and the freight yard gave way to summer cabins and resort traffic, and the town settled into the quieter shape it still has. Our short history of Boulder Creek follows that longer arc.

The trees that were saved

The logging story has a second ending, nine miles up the road. By 1900, most of Big Basin's old growth was privately owned, and Whaley's Big Basin history notes that businessman Henry L. Middleton had bought up the majority of the basin intending to harvest it. That May, a group led by San Jose photographer Andrew P. Hill camped in the basin, formed the Sempervirens Club, and passed a hat that collected 32 dollars to start the campaign. Two years of lobbying produced a state bill in 1901, and in 1902 California bought 3,800 acres of ancient forest, which the Sempervirens Fund calls the first park established in the current state park system.

Big Basin Redwoods State Park now preserves more than 18,000 acres, including the largest continuous stand of ancient coast redwoods south of San Francisco. The 2020 CZU fire burned through 97 percent of the park, but most of the old growth survived, which says something about what those trees have already lived through. The full preservation story is in our article on how Big Basin became California's first state park.

What you can still see today

The logging era hides in plain sight around town. The San Lorenzo Valley Museum at 12547 Highway 9 keeps the photographs, tools, and records of the mill years, and it's the right first stop if this article leaves you wanting more. Along the river between Ben Lomond and Boulder Creek, concrete piers and sawed-off pilings from the railroad still stand in and beside the water, though most of the old right-of-way now runs through private property, so look from public road crossings rather than wandering the grade. And the second-growth redwoods on every ridge around town are the boom's most visible legacy. Nearly all of them sprouted from the stumps the loggers left, which means the forest you drive through on Highway 9 is itself an artifact of the timber years, still quietly repairing the damage.

Frequently Asked Questions

When was Boulder Creek's logging era?

Commercial milling started in the early 1860s with Joseph Wilbur Peery's small sawmill, but the industrial boom ran from the railroad's arrival in April 1885 until about 1915, when the accessible big timber was largely gone. The last freight train left town in January 1934.

How did lumber get out of the valley before the railroad?

From 1875 to 1885, a twelve-mile V-shaped flume floated rough-cut lumber from the upper San Lorenzo Valley down to Felton. It leaked, ran short of water in summer, and couldn't handle the valley's output, so the South Pacific Coast Railroad replaced it with the rail line that reached Boulder Creek in 1885.

Was all the old-growth forest around Boulder Creek cut down?

Most of it. The Sempervirens Fund estimates roughly 300 square miles of ancient redwood in the Santa Cruz Mountains were logged in the late 1800s. The major surviving old growth near Boulder Creek is in Big Basin, which the state began protecting with a 3,800-acre purchase in 1902.

What was the Dougherty Extension Railroad?

A private narrow-gauge logging railroad the Dougherty brothers built north from Boulder Creek starting in 1887 for the Santa Clara Valley Mill & Lumber Company. It grew to roughly eight and a half miles and served several mills upriver. Service ended around 1915 and the rails were sold for scrap in 1917.

Where can I see remnants of the logging era?

The San Lorenzo Valley Museum in Boulder Creek covers the period in depth, and concrete piers and cut-off pilings from the rail line are still visible along the San Lorenzo River, mostly viewable from public crossings since much of the old route is private property. Big Basin's old growth shows what the whole range once looked like.

title: Mountain Roads, Parking, and When Highway 9 Gets Complicated
excerpt: If you're driving to Boulder Creek, here's the short version: Highway 9 is a genuine mountain road, narrow and winding under the redwoods, and it asks for more patience than speed.
bodyHtml:
If you're driving to Boulder Creek, here's the short version: Highway 9 is a genuine mountain road, narrow and winding under the redwoods, and it asks for more patience than speed. Check conditions before you leave on Caltrans QuickMap or our Mountain Status page, expect cyclists on every curve, plan for small-town parking rather than big-lot parking, and don't count on cell signal the whole way up. None of that should scare you off. It's a beautiful drive. It just goes better for everyone, visitors and locals alike, when you know what kind of road you're on before you're on it.

This page covers the durable patterns, the things that are true most days. For what's happening right now, always go to the live sources linked throughout, because we can't promise you an open road or an empty parking spot, and neither can anyone else.

Know Whose Road You're On

Two highways define driving here, and both are state routes maintained by Caltrans. Highway 9 runs the length of the San Lorenzo Valley, connecting Santa Cruz to Felton, Ben Lomond, Brookdale, and Boulder Creek before climbing over the ridge toward Saratoga. Highway 236 splits off from downtown Boulder Creek and loops through Big Basin Redwoods State Park. For either route, you can check current conditions by entering the route number at Caltrans' road information page, calling 1-800-427-7623, or opening QuickMap, which shows closures and incidents in real time.

Nearly everything else, the smaller roads that branch off the highway, is maintained by Santa Cruz County, and those closures show up on the county's Road Closure Dashboard instead. If you come across a tree down or a flooded stretch on a county road, the county's 24-hour road dispatch is (831) 477-3999. Knowing which agency owns the road you're on saves you a phone transfer, and our emergency contacts page keeps all of these numbers in one place. For anything dangerous happening right now, call 911.

What Driving Highway 9 Is Actually Like

Highway 9 between Santa Cruz and Boulder Creek is about thirty minutes of continuous curves through redwood shade. The trees make it darker than you expect at midday and slick with needles and moisture long after rain has stopped. Sight lines are short, shoulders are thin or missing, and passing opportunities are rare, so the pace of the whole road is set by its slowest vehicle. Build that into your schedule and the drive is a pleasure instead of a frustration.

If that slowest vehicle is you, that's fine, but use the turnouts. California law, Vehicle Code 21656, requires a slow-moving vehicle on a two-lane road to pull into the next turnout or safe area whenever five or more vehicles stack up behind it. Locals use the turnouts reflexively and notice, kindly or otherwise, who doesn't. Pulling over for ten seconds is the single most neighborly thing a visitor can do on this road.

A few other habits that fit the road: don't stop in the travel lane for photos, even briefly, since the next driver comes around the curve blind. Use your headlights in the redwood shade. And leave the shortcuts to the map apps alone in bad weather, because the side roads they suggest are narrower, steeper, and slower than the highway they're avoiding.

Sharing the Curves with Cyclists

Highway 9 is one of the classic road cycling climbs in the Bay Area, and you will meet riders on it, especially on weekend mornings. The law here is specific. California's Three Feet for Safety Act, Vehicle Code 21760, requires drivers to give at least three feet of clearance when passing a bicycle, and when three feet isn't possible, to slow down and pass only when it won't endanger the rider.

On a road with this many blind curves, that mostly means waiting. Hang back at a comfortable distance, wait for a straight stretch or a turnout, then pass with room to spare. The wait is rarely more than a minute, and it beats meeting an oncoming car mid-pass with a cyclist on your right. Riders, for their part, tend to use the turnouts too when a line forms; the road works because most people extend each other that courtesy.

Parking in Boulder Creek

Boulder Creek's downtown is a few blocks of Highway 9, and parking is small-town scale: street parking along the highway and side streets, plus modest lots serving individual businesses. On an ordinary weekday you'll likely find a spot within a short walk. On sunny summer weekends, holiday events, and festival days, the easy spots go early, so arrive on the early side or be ready to park a few blocks out and walk.

A few requests from the people who live here. Watch for posted signs and don't block driveways, fire hydrants, or the fire station's approach on Central Avenue. Don't park in business lots you're not patronizing. Leave room at trailheads and turnouts for residents whose driveways share them, and never park where your car narrows the road itself, because fire engines and delivery trucks need every foot of width these roads have. If a spot feels questionable, it probably is.

Highway 236 and the Big Basin Run

The drive from downtown Boulder Creek to Big Basin Redwoods State Park is about 9 miles up Highway 236, and the park's own guidance says it plainly: all roads into Big Basin are curvy. Sections of 236 are effectively single-lane threading between old-growth trunks, so take it slow and expect to yield.

Two things to know before you commit to the drive. First, the park currently can't accommodate oversized vehicles or trailers for day-use parking, so leave the RV at camp. Second, day-use parking is limited and the park highly recommends reservations. Check the park page for current reservation details before you go, since post-fire recovery means conditions there keep evolving. When 236 has a problem, it shows up in the same Caltrans tools as Highway 9: QuickMap, the route lookup, or 1-800-427-7623.

Don't Bet on Your Phone

Cell coverage in the San Lorenzo Valley is real but patchy, and it drops out in stretches of the canyon along both highways, with dead pockets that vary by carrier. The practical response is simple: download offline maps before you leave, screenshot your destination and any reservation, and tell someone where you're headed if you're hiking. If your plans depend on calling ahead, make the call from town rather than the road. Coverage can also degrade in storms and outages, which is one more reason the paper-map habit never really died up here.

When Storms Change Everything

In winter, atmospheric river storms can close sections of Highway 9, Highway 236, and the county roads with little warning, through flooding, slides, or downed trees and lines. The durable advice is to check before you drive: the NWS Bay Area forecast for what's coming, QuickMap and the county Road Closure Dashboard for what's closed, and our Mountain Status page, which gathers the official links. If you reach water across the road, turn around. The National Weather Service warns that twelve inches of moving water can carry away most cars and that the road under floodwater may already be gone. Never drive around closure barriers; they're there because someone confirmed the road isn't safe.

Storm days are honestly not the days to visit. But if you're already here when weather rolls in, businesses in town stay friendly, and waiting out a squall with coffee beats testing a flooded dip in the road every time.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Highway 9 open right now?

We never answer that from a static page, and you shouldn't trust any page that does. Check Caltrans QuickMap, enter route 9 at the Caltrans road information page, or call 1-800-427-7623. Our Mountain Status page links all of the live sources in one place.

Can I drive an RV or trailer to Big Basin?

Not for day use right now. The park's official page says no oversized vehicles or trailers can be accommodated for day-use parking, and stretches of Highway 236 are narrow enough that big rigs struggle regardless. Park the RV elsewhere and drive in with a passenger vehicle, ideally with a parking reservation.

Where should I park for a day in downtown Boulder Creek?

Street parking on and just off Highway 9 is the main supply, with small business lots for customers. Come early on weekends and event days, obey posted signs, and don't block driveways, hydrants, or road width. There's no big central lot, so expect a short walk and treat it as part of the visit.

Will I have cell service on the drive up?

Sometimes, and you shouldn't plan around it. Coverage drops in stretches of the canyon on both highways and varies by carrier, so download offline maps, save what you need before leaving Santa Cruz or Saratoga, and let someone know your plans if you're heading into the parks.

What do I do if a tree is down across the road?

If wires are involved or it's blocking traffic dangerously, call 911 and keep your distance. Otherwise report it: (831) 477-3999 reaches Santa Cruz County's 24-hour road dispatch for county roads, and Caltrans handles Highways 9 and 236, reachable via roads.dot.ca.gov or 1-800-427-7623. Our emergency contacts list has the full set of numbers.

title: "The San Lorenzo Valley Museum: What to See and How to Support It"
excerpt: Here's what you need to know before anything else: the San Lorenzo Valley Museum is free, and it's open more than most people think.
bodyHtml:
Here's what you need to know before anything else: the San Lorenzo Valley Museum is free, and it's open more than most people think. The main Grace Episcopal Gallery at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek is open Wednesday through Sunday, 1 to 4 p.m. A second gallery, the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery at 6299 Gushee Street in Felton, is open Thursday, Saturday, and Sunday, same afternoon hours. Donations are appreciated, but nobody's standing at the door with a cash box.

And 2026 is the year to finally go, because the historical society behind the museum is turning 50. The whole year is built around that anniversary, with a retrospective exhibit, monthly heritage demonstrations, and a big dinner in August. Everything in this guide was checked against the museum's own site on July 15, 2026. Hours and programs do change, so confirm at slvmuseum.org before a special trip.

Two galleries, both former churches

The museum's odd superpower is its buildings. Both galleries are 19th- and early-20th-century churches, and both are listed on the National Register of Historic Places, which means the containers are exhibits too.

The Boulder Creek building is the original Grace Episcopal Church, a small Gothic Revival church built in 1906 and listed on the National Register in 2006 for its architecture: the steep gable roofs, pointed arches, and tracery you can still see from Highway 9. The historical society notes the church originally stood in what was the separate town of Lorenzo, a settlement south of Boulder Creek proper that faded away while its church survived. If you've read our short history of Boulder Creek, this is the building that closes that story.

The Felton gallery has a longer resume. It began as the Felton Presbyterian Church, dedicated in 1893, then spent decades as Felton's public library under the name Faye G. Belardi Memorial Library before the library moved to a new building in 2019. It was listed on the National Register back in 1978. The historical society opened it as its second gallery in 2020, and it now carries the Belardi name forward as the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery.

Fifty years of the historical society

The museum didn't start with either building. The society was founded in Boulder Creek in March 1976 by three locals, Ralph Wilcox, Walt Bachrach, and John Holm, as the Boulder Creek Historical Society. For about twenty years its museum lived in a second-floor office downtown, the kind of operation you'd only find if you knew to look.

December 1999 changed that, when the society opened the museum in the renovated Grace Episcopal Church building. In 2012 the organization renamed itself the San Lorenzo Valley Historical Society to match the territory it actually covers, from Boulder Creek down through Brookdale, Ben Lomond, and Felton. The Felton gallery followed in 2020, and the two-gallery setup lets the museum run a long-term exhibit in one building while rotating shows through the other.

What's on in 2026

The anniversary year has a full calendar. As of mid-July 2026, here's what the museum has posted:

Retrospect, at the Grace gallery in Boulder Creek through late 2026, looks back at the historical society's own first 50 years. It's the anniversary show, and a good first visit if you've never been.

The Road I Call Home, running through September 20, 2026, brings photographer Randy Bacon's portrait work documenting 32 people experiencing homelessness. It's a heavier show than the museum's usual fare, and worth your time.

Americana is an open call for submissions: the museum is asking local artists for 2D work on American cultural themes, with submissions accepted August 1 through 31, 2026. If you make art in the valley, this is your invitation.

Heritage Demonstrations run monthly from April through August on the third Saturday, with hands-on demonstrations of the skills that built the valley.

The centerpiece is the 50th Anniversary Dinner & Auction on Saturday, August 8, 2026, at Scopazzi's in Boulder Creek, one of the town's longest-running restaurants and a fitting room for it. (If dinner out is your angle, our guide to eating in Boulder Creek covers Scopazzi's and everyone else.) Tickets and details are on the museum site.

The archives are the deep end

The galleries are what most visitors see, but the archives are what make the museum matter to anyone researching a house, a family, or a piece of valley history. The collection at the Boulder Creek building includes local newspapers from the 1890s to the present, business and organizational records, maps, forestry archives, family records, and roughly 5,000 photographs in the Fredda C. Carr collection, shot around the valley between 1950 and 1970.

Access is by appointment: call (831) 338-8382 or email archives@slvmuseum.org. There's also an online research request form if you'd rather the staff dig for you. The policies are friendly by archive standards. Non-flash photography of materials is allowed, and the museum will provide scans for non-commercial use on request.

The society also keeps a reference library of about 900 local and regional history books at the Felton gallery, browsable during regular hours or by appointment through library@slvmuseum.org, with the full catalog searchable online through LibraryThing.

How to support it

Free admission is a gift, and it only works if enough people give back. Four honest ways to do that, in rough order of commitment:

Visit and drop something in the donation box. The lowest bar, and it counts.

Become a member. Membership and donations run through Zeffy links on the museum's site. Members get a 15 percent discount in the museum store and the printed newsletter. The site has current levels and pricing.

Volunteer as a docent. Docents welcome visitors at both galleries, handle the bookstore, and guide people through the exhibits. The museum asks for a one-year commitment at a minimum of three hours a month, provides training, and gives volunteers a one-year individual membership plus a volunteers-only recognition event. For a three-hour monthly shift, that's a fair trade.

Adopt a native plant bed. The Grace gallery grounds double as Flora, a living exhibition of native plants and their historic uses, and the museum runs an Adopt-a-Native-Plant-Bed program for people who'd rather contribute with a trowel than a checkbook.

If you can't make it in person

The museum's Museum From Home page is better than most small museums manage. There are virtual exhibits on women's suffrage in Santa Cruz County, valley ingenuity ("Gadgets & Gizmos"), the San Lorenzo Valley's Chinese community, and grizzly bear encounters, plus a feature on Google Arts & Culture, a photograph and postcard collection on Flickr, and an active blog. It's a good rainy-week rabbit hole, and a good preview before you visit.

One housekeeping note: the museum's site is slvmuseum.org. The .com version of the address redirects there, so either gets you to the right place, but the .org is the one to save.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does the San Lorenzo Valley Museum cost?

Nothing. Admission to both galleries is free, and donations are appreciated. Memberships and donations fund the operation, so if you visit regularly, joining is the fair-play move.

When is the museum open?

As of July 2026: the Grace Episcopal Gallery in Boulder Creek is open Wednesday through Sunday, 1 to 4 p.m., and the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery in Felton is open Thursday, Saturday, and Sunday, 1 to 4 p.m. The office keeps Wednesday through Saturday hours. Check slvmuseum.org before you go, since hours change with seasons and staffing.

Can the museum help me research my house or family?

Yes, that's what the archives are for. Access is by appointment at (831) 338-8382 or archives@slvmuseum.org, and there's an online research request form. Holdings include newspapers back to the 1890s, maps, business records, family records, and thousands of photographs.

Which gallery should I visit first?

If you're in Boulder Creek, start with the Grace Episcopal Gallery, both for the 1906 building and for the Retrospect anniversary exhibit running through late 2026. The Felton gallery is the natural second stop, and it holds the reference library.

How do I volunteer?

Contact the museum at (831) 338-8382 or info@slvmuseum.org. Docents commit to at least a year at three hours a month, training included, and get a free membership out of it.

title: The Mountain Home Maintenance Calendar
excerpt: Owning a home in Boulder Creek runs on two seasons, not four: the wet one and the dry one.
bodyHtml:
Owning a home in Boulder Creek runs on two seasons, not four: the wet one and the dry one. Get your drainage, roof, and trees ready before the rains, and your defensible space and generator ready before fire season, and most of the year takes care of itself. This calendar lays out that rhythm month by month. One honest caveat before we start: every parcel up here is different. A house on a sunny ridge and a cabin in a creek-bottom redwood grove have different problems, so treat this as the rhythm, not a rulebook, and lean on the pros and the official sources linked below for anything specific to your property.

January and February: Storm Season, Eyes Open

Deep winter is less about projects and more about watching your systems work. During and after each big storm, walk the property when it's safe to do so. Check that gutters are overflowing nowhere, that downspouts are moving water away from the foundation, and that the ditches and culverts that drain your driveway are running clear. A culvert that plugs mid-January is how driveways disappear, and clearing leaves now is far cheaper than repairing washout later.

Keep an eye on the trees you flagged in fall. Saturated ground plus wind is when limbs and whole trees come down. If anything falls into power lines, stay away and call 911; our emergency contacts list has the numbers for everything less urgent, including county road dispatch at (831) 477-3999.

Indoors, make this the season you're strict about alarms. The U.S. Fire Administration says to test smoke alarms monthly, whatever type you have, and if anything in your house burns fuel or you run a generator, a carbon monoxide detector near every sleeping area is non-negotiable. The CDC counts more than 400 American deaths a year from CO, a gas you can't see or smell.

March and April: Assess the Damage, Start the Fire Work

When the storm train slows down, take stock. Look at the roof from the ground with binoculars for lifted shingles, debris dams, and moss that holds moisture against the roofing. Check decks, railings, and stairs for winter rot. Note where water pooled or cut channels this winter and fix the grading or drainage while the memory is fresh; the county's storm preparedness page links a good home drainage guide if you want a starting point.

This is also when defensible space season begins in earnest, while the vegetation is still green and soft. CAL FIRE's defensible space guidance organizes the work in zones: the first 5 feet from the house should be ember-resistant, with hardscape instead of combustible mulch and nothing dead or dry against the structure; within 30 feet, keep things lean, clean, and green; and out to 100 feet or your property line, reduce fuel, with grass mowed to four inches or less. State law, Public Resources Code 4291, requires 100 feet of defensible space, and it's a year-round obligation, not a one-day deadline. Spring is simply when the work is most pleasant and the growth is easiest to cut.

May and June: Finish Defensible Space Before the Heat

By late spring, the goal is to have the heavy defensible space work done: dead vegetation removed, tree branches limbed up at least 6 feet from the ground per CAL FIRE, wood piles relocated out of the first 30 feet, and the roof and gutters cleared of needles again, because they're ember beds in summer and dams in winter. CAL FIRE and its partners conduct defensible space inspections in the State Responsibility Area that covers much of our valley, so if an inspector visits, this is the work they're looking at. Don't count on a warning before an inspection, and don't need one.

June is also generator month, because Public Safety Power Shutoff season arrives with the dry winds. Start the generator, run it under load, check your fuel supply, and reread the two safety rules that matter most: outdoors only, more than 20 feet from windows, doors, and vents per the CDC, and never plugged into house wiring without a proper transfer switch. Your owner's manual sets the maintenance schedule; the habit of testing before you need it is the part we'll insist on. The whole setup is covered in our power outage plan.

July and August: Vigilance, Water, and the Small Stuff

High summer is maintenance-light and awareness-heavy. Keep the first five feet around the house bare of dry debris, since redwood duff keeps falling all summer. Confirm your CruzAware alert registration is current, especially if you've changed phones or carriers, and make sure your PG&E outage alerts are set.

Use the slow months for the unglamorous items. Refresh the emergency kit against ready.gov's list, rotating stored water, one gallon per person per day for several days, and swapping out expired food and medications. If you're on a private well, summer is a sensible time for any pump or tank service, when a day without water is an annoyance instead of a crisis. If you're on district water, the San Lorenzo Valley Water District at (831) 338-2153 can answer system questions; our utilities guide sorts out who serves what.

September and October: The Big Push Before the Rains

This is the most important stretch of the calendar, and the one where procrastination costs real money. Before the first atmospheric river arrives, clean gutters and downspouts, clear every ditch, drain, and culvert you own, and get roof repairs done while roofers can still get to you. Walk the property and deal with dead limbs over the house, cars, and power drops; tree crews book out quickly once storms are in the forecast, so call early. If your place heats with a wood stove, have the stove and chimney serviced before burning season starts.

Two more fall jobs. First, if you heat with propane, get the tank filled before winter, when delivery schedules tighten for everyone. Second, restock the storm side of your kit: flashlights, batteries, a radio, and sandbags if your parcel needs them, which the county distributes at posted locations each storm season via the storm preparedness page. Our winter storm checklist turns this whole section into a single afternoon's work.

November and December: Switch to Storm Mode

By Thanksgiving, you want to be done preparing and simply operating. Keep vehicles fueled ahead of big systems, check drainage after each of the first storms, and keep the generator's fuel fresh and its extension cords where you can find them in the dark. Test the smoke and CO alarms with the monthly routine, and if you have a 9-volt-battery alarm, the USFA says replace that battery at least once a year, so pick a date you'll remember and make it annual.

December is also a good month for the paperwork nobody enjoys: photograph the house and outbuildings for insurance, back up the photos somewhere off the mountain, and confirm your policy reflects what's actually on the parcel.

The Recurring Beat, All Year

A few items ignore the seasons. Test smoke alarms monthly and replace any alarm that's 10 years old, per the USFA. If you're on septic, like most of Boulder Creek, the EPA says to have the system inspected at least every three years and expect to pump the tank every three to five, with yearly checks for systems that have pumps or floats. Put both on a calendar you'll actually see, because neither one reminds you until it fails, and a failed drainfield is one of the most expensive surprises a mountain house can serve up.

Frequently Asked Questions

When is the deadline for defensible space work?

There isn't a single statewide date. CAL FIRE frames the 100 feet of defensible space required by Public Resources Code 4291 as an ongoing obligation, maintained year-round, and its crews inspect properties in the State Responsibility Area on their own schedule. Practically, do the heavy cutting in spring and keep it maintained through fire season.

How often should a septic system be pumped in Boulder Creek?

The EPA's guidance is an inspection at least every three years and pumping typically every three to five years, more often for systems with mechanical parts. Your actual interval depends on tank size and household size, so ask your septic professional to put a recommendation in writing after the next inspection.

What's the most important maintenance month of the year?

October, and it's not close. Gutters, culverts, roof, trees, and storm supplies all need to be squared away before the first big rain, and every trade you might need is busiest right then. If you only ever plan one maintenance weekend a year, make it early October and work from our winter storm checklist.

Do I really need to test smoke alarms every month?

That's the U.S. Fire Administration's standing guidance for every alarm type: test monthly, replace 9-volt and backup batteries at least yearly, and replace the whole unit every 10 years. In a wooden house surrounded by forest, with winter fires burning and generators running, it's cheap discipline.

My property doesn't match this calendar. What then?

That's normal up here. Slope, sun, trees, water source, and road access change the priorities parcel by parcel, and this calendar can't see your land. Use it as the skeleton, then let a good local arborist, septic professional, and the official sources linked above fill in the specifics for your place.

title: Downtown Boulder Creek in an Afternoon
excerpt: An afternoon is the right amount of time for downtown Boulder Creek, and here's the short version: browse the shops along Highway 9 and Central Avenue, catch the free San Lorenzo Valley Museum between 1 and 4, put your feet in the river at Junction Park, and eat before you leave.
bodyHtml:
An afternoon is the right amount of time for downtown Boulder Creek, and here's the short version: browse the shops along Highway 9 and Central Avenue, catch the free San Lorenzo Valley Museum between 1 and 4, put your feet in the river at Junction Park, and eat before you leave. Everything named below was confirmed operating on July 15, 2026, against its own website, because search results for this town still surface businesses that closed years ago. This isn't a ranking and nothing here is rated; it's what's actually open, in the order an afternoon naturally takes it.

One framing note before you come up the hill. Boulder Creek is a working mountain town of homes, hardware, and groceries, not a resort built for you.
